note_data.title: Vectors
keyPoints: Vector — A physical quantity which has both magnitude and direction is called a vector
Scalar — A physical quantity which has only magnitude but no direction is called a scalar
Vectors are represented by arrows where length shows magnitude and arrowhead shows direction
Addition of vectors follows triangle law and parallelogram law
Resultant vector — The single vector which produces the same effect as produced by a number of vectors jointly
Component of vector — The perpendicular projection of a vector along any direction is called component
Unit vector — A vector of unit magnitude in any direction is called unit vector
Vectors can be added graphically by head-to-tail method or parallelogram method
Analytical method uses trigonometry to find resultant using Pythagorean theorem

definitions.definition: A physical quantity which has both magnitude and direction is called a vector
A physical quantity which has only magnitude but no direction is called a scalar
The single vector which produces the same effect as produced by a number of vectors jointly is called resultant vector
A vector of unit magnitude in any direction is called unit vector
The perpendicular projection of a vector along any direction is called component of vector

importantQuestions.question: Differentiate between scalar and vector quantities with examples.
importantQuestions.answer: Scalar has only magnitude (mass, time, temperature). Vector has both magnitude and direction (displacement, velocity, force). Scalars are added algebraically while vectors are added using special laws.

importantQuestions.question: State and explain parallelogram law of vector addition. Derive expression for resultant.
importantQuestions.answer: Parallelogram Law: If two vectors are represented by two adjacent sides of a parallelogram, then their resultant is represented by the diagonal of parallelogram. Derivation: R² = A² + B² + 2AB cosθ, where θ is angle between vectors A and B. Direction: tan α = B sinθ/(A + B cosθ)

mcqs.question: Which of the following is a vector quantity?
The magnitude of resultant of two equal vectors is maximum when angle between them is:

commonMistakes: Students confuse scalar and vector - remember vectors need both magnitude AND direction
Forgetting to draw arrowheads on vector diagrams
Using simple addition for vectors instead of vector addition laws
Not converting angle to correct quadrant in analytical method
Mixing up sine and cosine in component calculations
examTips: This chapter usually has 1-2 MCQs and 1 short question in every BSEK paper
Parallelogram law derivation appears almost every year - memorize complete steps
Always draw proper vector diagrams with arrows and labels
Learn examples of scalar and vector quantities by heart
Practice analytical method problems with different angles
formulas: tan α = B sinθ/(A + B cosθ) (Direction of resultant)
Ax = A cosθ, Ay = A sinθ (Components)
note_data.keyDiagram: Parallelogram diagram showing two vectors A and B as adjacent sides, resultant R as diagonal, with angle θ between vectors and angle α for direction of resultant. Must include proper arrowheads and labels.
pastPaperRefs: BSEK 2023 SSC-II Q3 (4 marks) — asked students to define scalar and vector quantities with two examples each
BSEK 2022 SSC-II Q7 (6 marks) — required addition of two vectors using parallelogram law with diagram
BSEK 2024 SSC-II Q4 (3 marks) — asked to differentiate between displacement and distance as vector and scalar
BSEK 2021 SSC-II Q8 (5 marks) — demanded resolution of forces into rectangular components with calculations

workedExample.question: Define vector quantity and find the resultant of two forces 3N and 4N acting at right angles to each other. (5 marks)
steps: Define vector quantity using STBB textbook definition
Draw diagram showing two perpendicular forces
Apply Pythagorean theorem: R² = F₁² + F₂²
workedExample.answer: Vector quantity is a physical quantity which has both magnitude and direction. Examples: force, velocity, displacement. Resultant = 5N at angle θ = tan⁻¹(4/3) = 53.13° with 3N force.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for exact definition, 1 mark for diagram, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for calculation, 1 mark for final answer with direction

examinerWarnings: Students often lose marks by writing general answers instead of textbook-exact definitions. Always use the STBB textbook phrasing for vector and scalar definitions.
Never confuse distance with displacement or speed with velocity - clearly state which is scalar and which is vector with proper reasoning.
When drawing vector diagrams, always use arrows and label magnitudes clearly. Missing arrows or unlabeled vectors lose marks in BSEK papers.
note_data.quickRecap: Vector quantities have both magnitude and direction (force, velocity, displacement) while scalar quantities have only magnitude (distance, speed, mass). Vectors can be added using triangle law, parallelogram law, or rectangular components method. Always remember to include direction in final answers and use proper vector notation with arrows in diagrams.
importantFormulas: Resultant of perpendicular vectors: R = √(A² + B²)
Components: Fx = F cos θ, Fy = F sin θ
